text: Linked List Pattern: Reverse linked list. Iterative: prev=None, curr=head. While curr: next=curr.next; curr.next=prev; prev=curr; curr=next. Return prev. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Recursive: if not head or not head.next: return head; new_head=reverse(head.next); head.next.next=head; head.next=None; return new_head. Time: O(n), Space: O(n) stack.

Linked List Pattern: Detect cycle (Floyd's algorithm). Slow pointer moves 1 step, fast moves 2. If they meet, cycle exists. To find cycle start: reset slow to head, keep fast at meeting point, advance both 1 step at a time. They meet at cycle start. Proof: if cycle length is C and meeting point is k steps into cycle, the math shows they'll meet at cycle start. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

Linked List Pattern: Find middle node. Slow and fast pointers. Slow moves 1, fast moves 2. When fast reaches end, slow is at middle. For even-length list: slow lands on first middle (fast.next == None) or second middle (fast == None) depending on initialization. Use for: palindrome linked list check (find middle, reverse second half), merge sort on linked list.

Linked List Pattern: Merge two sorted lists. Two pointers, one per list. Compare heads, attach smaller. Recurse or iterate. Iterative: dummy head to simplify edge cases. curr = dummy; while l1 and l2: if l1.val <= l2.val: curr.next=l1; l1=l1.next; else: curr.next=l2; l2=l2.next; curr=curr.next; curr.next = l1 or l2. Time: O(m+n), Space: O(1).

Linked List Pattern: Remove Nth node from end. One-pass with two pointers. Advance fast pointer N steps ahead. Move both until fast reaches end. Slow.next is the target. Remove: slow.next = slow.next.next. Use dummy head to handle edge cases (removing head). Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Alternative: find length, then remove at len-N+1, requires two passes.

Linked List Pattern: Merge K sorted lists. Method 1: min-heap of (val, list_index, node). Pop min, push next from same list. Time: O(n log k). Method 2: divide and conquer — merge pairs, then pairs of pairs. Log k rounds, each O(n). Time: O(n log k). Method 3: merge one by one — O(nk). Method 1 and 2 are equivalent asymptotically; Method 2 avoids heap overhead.

Linked List Pattern: Copy list with random pointer. Two-pass with hash map. Pass 1: create copy of each node, store in map (original → copy). Pass 2: assign next and random pointers using map. Time: O(n), Space: O(n). O(1) space approach: interleave original and copies, set random pointers, separate lists. Space: O(1) but modifies and restores original.

Linked List Pattern: Add two numbers (represented as linked lists). Lists represent digits in reverse order. Traverse both simultaneously, add with carry. Create new node for each digit sum % 10, carry = sum // 10. Continue while either list has nodes or carry > 0. Handle different lengths with 0 padding. Time: O(max(m,n)), Space: O(max(m,n)).

Linked List Pattern: Reorder list (L0→Ln→L1→Ln-1→...). Find middle (slow/fast pointers). Reverse second half. Merge two halves alternately. Merge: while both exist, interleave nodes. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Key: reverse in-place and merge without extra space. Breaking list at middle and reversing second half is the core insight.

Linked List Pattern: Remove duplicates from sorted list. Adjacent equal nodes: skip duplicates. Iterative: if curr.val == curr.next.val: curr.next = curr.next.next; else: curr=curr.next. For remove ALL nodes with duplicates (not just extras): use prev pointer, skip entire run of duplicates, connect prev to first non-duplicate after run. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

Linked List Pattern: Rotate list by K positions. Connect tail to head (circular). Find new tail at position len-k%len-1. New head = new_tail.next. Break cycle: new_tail.next = None. Handle k%len==0 (no rotation needed). Time: O(n). Key: first find list length to handle k > len and find the cut point efficiently.

Linked List Pattern: Partition list around value X. All nodes less than X before nodes >= X. Maintain two dummy lists: less_list and greater_list. Traverse, append each node to appropriate list. Connect less_list tail to greater_list head. Set greater_list tail.next=None. Time: O(n), Space: O(1) (reuses nodes). Stable partitioning preserves relative order.

Linked List Pattern: LRU Cache implementation. Doubly linked list + hash map. List maintains order (most recently used at head, least at tail). Map provides O(1) access by key. get: if exists, move to head, return value. put: if exists, update and move to head; if new, add to head; if over capacity, remove tail. Time: O(1) for both operations. Space: O(capacity).

Linked List Pattern: Intersection of two linked lists. Find node where two lists merge. Length approach: find lengths, advance longer list by difference, walk together until meeting. Two-pointer approach: a and b start at respective heads; when one reaches end, restart at other list's head. They meet at intersection or both reach None simultaneously. Time: O(m+n), Space: O(1).

Linked List Pattern: Palindrome linked list check. Find middle (slow/fast). Reverse second half from middle. Compare first half with reversed second half. Optionally restore list after check. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Alternative: copy to array, two-pointer check O(n) time O(n) space. The O(1) space approach modifies the list temporarily.

Linked List Pattern: Sort linked list. Merge sort is optimal: find middle, split, recursively sort halves, merge. Time: O(n log n), Space: O(log n) stack. QuickSort on linked list: O(n²) worst case, hard to pivot efficiently. Insertion sort: O(n²). Merge sort is standard for linked list sorting because accessing middle via slow/fast pointer is O(n) — no random access.

Linked List Pattern: Flatten multilevel doubly linked list. Each node may have a child list. DFS: when child encountered, insert child list between current and next. Process recursively. Iterative: use stack. Push next (if exists), process child list. Time: O(n), Space: O(depth) for recursion or stack. Sentinel/dummy head simplifies edge cases when inserting.

Linked List Pattern: Linked list cycle length. After detecting meeting point (Floyd's), keep one pointer fixed, advance other until they meet again — count steps = cycle length. Or: from meeting point, advance one pointer and count steps until back to meeting point. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Use for: Rho factorization, pseudorandom sequence period detection.

Linked List Pattern: Reverse nodes in K-group. Reverse every K nodes. For each group: check if K nodes exist (count or find kth node). Reverse K nodes using prev/next tracking. Connect previous group's tail to new group head. Template: while group of K exists: reverse K nodes, advance. Handle remaining nodes (leave as is or reverse partial group). Time: O(n).

Linked List Pattern: Sentinel/dummy head node. Create dummy node before actual head. Simplifies edge cases: deleting head, inserting before head, empty list handling. All operations work uniformly without special casing head. After processing: return dummy.next as actual result. Applicable to: merge, partition, remove elements, create new linked list. Minimal overhead, significant code simplification.
